revision.comment: 詳しい方修正お願いします
revision.text:
'''内舎人（うどねり）'''は[[律令制]]における官職のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と天皇の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、時期により増減がある。内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の随身を務めたこともあり、これを[['''内舎人随身''']]と呼ぶ。[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。
[[武士]]が登場すると武士が任ぜられることが多くなる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''藤内'''と呼んだ。
[[歌人]]として有名な[[大伴家持]]も内舎人を務めたことがある。
== 関連項目 ==
*[[日本の官制]]
{{history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制|うとねり]] [[category:日本の皇室|うとねり]]

『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と天皇の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、時期により増減がある。内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の随身を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。
[[武士]]が登場すると武士が任ぜられることが多くなる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''藤内'''と呼んだ。
[[歌人]]として有名な[[大伴家持]]も内舎人を務めたことがある。

『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、時期により増減がある。内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の随身を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。
[[武士]]が登場すると武士が任ぜられることが多くなる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''藤内'''と呼んだ。
[[歌人]]として有名な[[大伴家持]]も内舎人を務めたことがある。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。 == 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は[[律令制]]における官職のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、時期により増減がある。内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の随身を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。
[[武士]]が登場すると武士が任ぜられることが多くなる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''藤内'''と呼んだ。
[[歌人]]として有名な[[大伴家持]]も内舎人を務めたことがある。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は[[律令制]]における官職のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、{{和暦|808}}に40人に削減されるなど、時期により増減がある。内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった。
[[武士]]が登場すると武士が任ぜられることが多くなる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''藤内'''と呼んだ。
[[歌人]]として有名な[[大伴家持]]も内舎人を務めたことがある。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は[[律令制]]における官職のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、{{和暦|808}}に40人に削減されていたが、[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため、{{和暦|1147}}に定員が60名に増やされたが、[[12世紀]]末には実際には100名以上置かれている（『[[官職秘抄]]』）など、時期により増減がある。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった。
[[武士]]が登場すると武士が任ぜられることが多くなる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''藤内'''と呼んだ。
[[歌人]]として有名な[[大伴家持]]も内舎人を務めたことがある。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は[[律令制]]における官職のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、{{和暦|808}}に40人に削減されていたが、[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため、{{和暦|1148}}に定員が60名に増やされたが、[[12世紀]]末には実際には100名以上置かれている（『[[官職秘抄]]』）など、時期により増減がある。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった。
[[武士]]が登場すると武士が任ぜられることが多くなる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]で任ぜられた者を'''藤内'''と呼んだ。
[[歌人]]として有名な[[大伴家持]]も内舎人を務めたことがある。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は[[律令制]]における官職のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、{{和暦|808}}に40人に削減されていたが、[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため、{{和暦|1148}}に定員が60名に増やされたが、[[12世紀]]末には実際には100名以上置かれている（『[[官職秘抄]]』）など、時期により増減がある。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった。
武士が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は[[律令制]]における官職のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である<ref>和田英松著、所功校訂『官職要解』（岩波書店、2006年）73頁参照。</ref>。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、{{和暦|808}}に40人に削減されていたが、[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため、{{和暦|1148}}に定員が60名に増やされたが、[[12世紀]]末には実際には100名以上置かれている（『[[官職秘抄]]』）など、時期により増減がある<ref name="wada74">和田英松前掲書（岩波書店、2006年）74頁参照。</ref>。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった<ref name="wada74"/>。
武士が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった<ref name="wada74"/>。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。

{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、{{和暦|808}}に40人に削減されていたが、[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため、{{和暦|1148}}に定員が60名に増やされたが、[[12世紀]]末には実際には100名以上置かれている（『[[官職秘抄]]』）など、時期により増減がある<ref name="wada74">和田英松前掲書（岩波書店、2006年）74頁参照。</ref>。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった<ref name="wada74"/>。
武士が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった<ref name="wada74"/>。

{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は[[律令制]]における[[官職]]のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である<ref>[[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『官職要解』（[[岩波書店]]、[[2006年]]）73頁参照。</ref>。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、[[808年]]（大同3年）に40人に削減されていたが、[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため、[[1148年]]（久安4年）に定員が60名に増やされたが、[[12世紀]]末には実際には100名以上置かれている（『[[官職秘抄]]』）など、時期により増減がある<ref name="wada74">和田英松前掲書（岩波書店、2006年）74頁参照。</ref>。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった<ref name="wada74"/>。
武士が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった<ref name="wada74"/>。
現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。
== 脚注 == <references/> == 参照文献 == * 和田英松著、所功校訂『官職要解』（岩波書店、2006年）ISBN 4061586211 == 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

現在も[[宮内庁]]の役職の一つである。

revision.text:
'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は宮中における役職。
== 律令制 == [[律令制]]における[[官職]]のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である<ref>[[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『官職要解』（[[岩波書店]]、[[2006年]]）73頁参照。</ref>。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、[[808年]]（大同3年）に40人に削減された。しかし[[1148年]]（久安4年）[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため定員が60名に増やされ、[[12世紀]]末になると実際には100名以上置かれていた（『[[官職秘抄]]』）。このように、人数については時期により増減がある<ref name="wada74">和田英松前掲書（岩波書店、2006年）74頁参照。</ref>。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下[[五位]]以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で任官された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の官職よりも昇進に有利であった<ref name="wada74"/>。

== 明治以降 == [[明治]]以降、[[宮内省]]に属することとなった。宮内省では[[侍従長]]が統括する侍従職に属した。

'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は、[[宮中]]における[[役職]]。
== 律令制 == [[律令制]]における[[官職]]のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である<ref>[[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『官職要解』（[[岩波書店]]、[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年））73頁参照。</ref>。
『[[大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、[[808年]]（[[大同 (日本)|大同]]3年）に40人に削減された。しかし[[1148年]]（[[久安]]4年）[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため定員が60名に増やされ、[[12世紀]]末になると実際には100名以上置かれていた（『[[官職秘抄]]』）。このように、人数については時期により増減がある<ref name="wada74">和田英松前掲書（岩波書店、2006年（平成18年））74頁参照。</ref>。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下五位以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で[[任官]]された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の[[官職]]よりも昇進に有利であった<ref name="wada74"/>。
[[武士]]が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった<ref name="wada74"/>。
== 明治時代以降 == [[明治]]時代以降、[[宮内省]]に属することとなった。宮内省では[[侍従|侍従長]]が統括する[[侍従職]]に属した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参照文献 == * [[和田英松]]著、所功校訂『官職要解』（[[岩波書店]]、[[2006年]]）ISBN 4061586211
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} [[category:律令制の官制]]

[[武士]]が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった<ref name="wada74"/>。
== 明治時代以降 == [[明治]]時代以降、[[宮内省]]に属することとなった。宮内省では[[侍従|侍従長]]が統括する[[侍従職]]に属した。

== 参照文献 == * [[和田英松]]著、所功校訂『官職要解』（[[岩波書店]]、[[2006年]]）ISBN 4061586211
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]

{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} [[Category:律令制の官制]]

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[imperial court]] position in Japan. == Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref> Accor..."
revision.text:
'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[808]] ([[Daido]] 3). However, in [[1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Ki clan|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[Lord Steward]] as part of the [[Board of Chamberlains]].
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2006, ISBN 4061586211.
== Related Articles == * [[Government of Ritsuryō Japan]] * [[Imperial Guards]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:Ritsuryō system]] [[ja:内舎人]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Dardar]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[808]] ([[Daido]] 3). However, in [[1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Ki clan|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[Lord Steward]] as part of the [[Board of Chamberlains]].
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2006, ISBN 4061586211.
== Related Articles == * [[Government of Ritsuryō Japan]] * [[Imperial Guards]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

revision.comment: 校閲。
revision.text:
'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は、[[宮中]]における[[役職]]。
== 律令制 == [[律令制]]における[[官職]]のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である<ref>[[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『官職要解』（[[岩波書店]]、[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年））73頁参照。</ref>。
『[[大宝律令|大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、[[808年]]（[[大同 (日本)|大同]]3年）に40人に削減された。しかし[[1148年]]（[[久安]]4年）[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため定員が60名に増やされ、[[12世紀]]末になると実際には100名以上置かれていた（『[[官職秘抄]]』）。このように、人数については時期により増減がある<ref name="wada74">和田英松前掲書（岩波書店、2006年（平成18年））74頁参照。</ref>。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下五位以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で[[任官]]された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の[[官職]]よりも昇進に有利であった<ref name="wada74"/>。
[[武士]]が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[大伴氏|伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった<ref name="wada74"/>。
== 明治時代以降 == [[明治]]時代以降、[[宮内省]]に属することとなった。宮内省では[[侍従|侍従長]]が統括する[[侍従職]]に属した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参照文献 == * [[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『新訂 官職要解』（[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[1983年]]）ISBN 4061586211
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} [[Category:律令制の官制]]

revision.text:
'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[808]] ([[Daido]] 3). However, in [[1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Ki clan|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[Lord Steward]] as part of the [[Board of Chamberlains]].
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2006, ISBN 4061586211.
== Related Articles == * [[Government of Ritsuryō Japan]] * [[Imperial Guards]]

Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[808]] ([[Daido]] 3). However, in [[1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>

Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. == Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2006, ISBN 4061586211.
== Related Articles == * [[Government of Ritsuryō Japan]] * [[Imperial Guards]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
[[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Ritsuryō system]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Udoneri]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:Udoneri]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Udoneri}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Udoneri]][[Category:11]] [[ja:内舎人]] [[Category:completion tier 4]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:completion tier 3]] [[Category:New]]

'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[808]] ([[Daido]] 3). However, in [[1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Ki clan|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[Lord Steward]] as part of the [[Board of Chamberlains]].
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2006, ISBN 4061586211.
== Related Articles == * [[Government of Ritsuryō Japan]] * [[Imperial Guards]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Ki clan|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.

revision.text:
'''内舎人'''（うどねり）は、[[宮中]]における[[役職]]。
== 律令制 == [[律令制]]における[[官職]]のひとつ。[[中務省]]に属する。『[[和名抄]]』では「'''うちとねり'''」と記載されている。「'''うどねり'''」は略称である<ref>[[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『官職要解』（[[岩波書店]]、[[2006年]]（[[平成]]18年））73頁参照。</ref>。
『[[大宝律令|大宝令]]』では帯刀宿衛、供奉雑使、駕行時の護衛と[[天皇]]の身辺警護にあたった。詰め所は中務省の東北隅。定員は90人であったが、[[808年]]（[[大同 (日本)|大同]]3年）に40人に削減された。しかし[[1148年]]（[[久安]]4年）[[成功 (任官)|成功]]の希望者に対応するため定員が60名に増やされ、[[12世紀]]末になると実際には100名以上置かれていた（『[[官職秘抄]]』）。このように、人数については時期により増減がある<ref name="wada74">和田英松前掲書（岩波書店、2006年（平成18年））74頁参照。</ref>。
内舎人から選抜された者が[[摂政]]、[[関白]]の[[随身]]を務めたこともあり、これを'''[[内舎人随身]]'''と呼ぶ。21歳以上の[[四位]]以下五位以上の子弟から選抜された。また、[[三位]]以上の子弟でも希望があれば無条件で[[任官]]された。[[長上]]の扱いを受けたために他の舎人系の[[官職]]よりも昇進に有利であった<ref name="wada74"/>。
[[武士]]が任ぜられることが多くなると、その本姓と「内舎人」を略した呼称が使われる。[[源氏]]で内舎人に任ぜられた者は'''源内'''、[[平氏]]ならば'''平内'''、[[藤原氏]]は'''藤内'''、[[橘氏]]は'''吉内'''、[[紀氏]]は'''喜内'''、[[清原氏]]は'''清内'''、[[大伴氏|伴氏]]は'''伴内'''のごとくである。後には、実際の内舎人職や本姓に関わらず名乗られるようになった<ref name="wada74"/>。
== 明治時代以降 == [[明治]]時代以降、[[宮内省]]に属することとなった。宮内省では[[侍従|侍従長]]が統括する[[侍従職]]に属した。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参照文献 == * [[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『新訂 官職要解』（[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[1983年]]）ISBN 4061586211
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:うとねり}} [[Category:律令制の官制]]

== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 参照文献 == * [[和田英松]]著、[[所功]]校訂『新訂 官職要解』（[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[1983年]]）ISBN 4061586211
== 関連項目 == * [[日本の官制]] * [[近衛兵]]

== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[808]] ([[Daido]] 3). However, in [[1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Ki clan|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[Lord Steward]] as part of the [[Board of Chamberlains]].

However, in [[:en:1148|1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[Lord Steward]] as part of the [[Board of Chamberlains]].

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[:en:808|808]] ([[:en:Daido|Daido]] 3). However, in [[:en:1148|1148]] ([[Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[Lord Steward]] as part of the [[:en:Board of Chamberlains|Board of Chamberlains]].
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), [[Iwanami Shoten]], 2006, ISBN 4061586211.
== Related Articles == * [[Government of Ritsuryō Japan]] * [[:en:Imperial Guards|Imperial Guards]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[:en:Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Lord Steward|Lord Steward]] as part of the [[:en:Board of Chamberlains|Board of Chamberlains]].

'''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[:en:Imperial court|Imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''[[Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[:en:808|808]] ([[:en:Daido|Daido]] 3). However, in [[:en:1148|1148]] ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[Kanshoku Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[:en:Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Lord Steward|Lord Steward]] as part of the [[:en:Board of Chamberlains|Board of Chamberlains]].

However, in [[:en:1148|1148]] ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[East Asian government position Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[:en:suitors|suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[:en:Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[:en:Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Lord Steward|Lord Steward]] as part of the [[:en:Board of Chamberlains|Board of Chamberlains]].

== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Lord Steward|Lord Steward]] as part of the [[:en:Board of Chamberlains|Board of Chamberlains]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内舎人]][[zh:內舍人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11394416]] Wikidata name: udoneri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[:en:Imperial court|Imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the [[:en:Ministry of the Center|Ministry of the Center]]. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]]'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the [[:en:Emperor of Japan|Emperor of Japan]], performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in [[:en:808|808]] ([[:en:Daido|Daido]] 3). However, in [[:en:1148|1148]] ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[East Asian government position Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[:en:suitors|suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As [[:en:Samurai|Samurai]] increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A [[:en:Tachibana clan|Tachibana]] Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A [[:en:Kiyohara clan|Kiyohara]] Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo]] Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the [[:en:Lord Steward|Lord Steward]] as part of the [[:en:Board of Chamberlains|Board of Chamberlains]].

In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>

However, in [[:en:1148|1148]] ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[East Asian government position Hishō]]''). Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内舎人]][[zh:內舍人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内舎人]][[zh:內舍人]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:内舎人]][[zh:內舍人]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11394416]] Wikidata name: udoneri [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:13, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Udoneri''' (内舎人) was an [[:en:Imperial court|Imperial court]] position in Japan.
== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center}}. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''{{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}}'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in {{ill|808|en|808|WD=Q399792|lt=808}} ([[:en:Daido|Daido]] 3). However, in {{ill|1148|en|1148|WD=Q19633|lt=1148}} ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[East Asian government position Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[:en:suitors|suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A {{ill|Tachibana clan|en|Tachibana clan|WD=Q7673718|lt=Tachibana}} Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A {{ill|Kiyohara clan|en|Kiyohara clan|WD=Q2975295|lt=Kiyohara}} Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo}} Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Lord Steward|en|Lord Steward|WD=Q1547759|lt=Lord Steward}} as part of the {{ill|Board of Chamberlains|en|Board of Chamberlains|WD=Q11453306|lt=Board of Chamberlains}}.
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 2006, ISBN 4061586211.
== Related Articles == * [[Government of Ritsuryō Japan]] * [[:en:Imperial Guards|Imperial Guards]]

== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center}}. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". The term "'''Udoneri'''" is an abbreviation.<ref>See [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by [[Isao Tokoro]], ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解) (Iwanami Shoten, 2006), p. 73.</ref>
According to the ''{{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|WD=Q1059892|lt=Taihō Code}}'', Udoneri were tasked with guarding the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|WD=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan}}, performing duties such as armed service, accompanying the Emperor, and serving as escorts during imperial processions. Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in {{ill|808|en|808|WD=Q399792|lt=808}} ([[:en:Daido|Daido]] 3). However, in {{ill|1148|en|1148|WD=Q19633|lt=1148}} ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[East Asian government position Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[:en:suitors|suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}} increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A {{ill|Tachibana clan|en|Tachibana clan|WD=Q7673718|lt=Tachibana}} Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A {{ill|Kiyohara clan|en|Kiyohara clan|WD=Q2975295|lt=Kiyohara}} Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo}} Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Lord Steward|en|Lord Steward|WD=Q1547759|lt=Lord Steward}} as part of the {{ill|Board of Chamberlains|en|Board of Chamberlains|WD=Q11453306|lt=Board of Chamberlains}}.
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten}}, 2006, ISBN 4061586211.

However, in {{ill|1148|en|1148|WD=Q19633|lt=1148|1=1148}} ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[East Asian government position Hishō]]''). The number of appointees varied over time.<ref name="wada74">Refer to Eimatsu Wada, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'', p. 74.</ref>
Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[:en:suitors|suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>
As {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} increasingly assumed these roles, the title "Udoneri" was often abbreviated and combined with the individual's surname. For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A {{ill|Tachibana clan|en|Tachibana clan|WD=Q7673718|lt=Tachibana|1=Tachibana clan}} Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A {{ill|Kiyohara clan|en|Kiyohara clan|WD=Q2975295|lt=Kiyohara|1=Kiyohara clan}} Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo|1=Ōtomo clan}} Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Lord Steward|en|Lord Steward|WD=Q1547759|lt=Lord Steward|1=Lord Steward}} as part of the {{ill|Board of Chamberlains|en|Board of Chamberlains|WD=Q11453306|lt=Board of Chamberlains|1=Board of Chamberlains}}.
== References == {{Footnotes}} {{Reflist}}
== Further Reading == * [[Eimatsu Wada]], edited by Isao Tokoro, ''Kan-shoku Yōkai'' (官職要解), {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|en|Iwanami Shoten|WD=Q1331427|lt=Iwanami Shoten|1=Iwanami Shoten}}, 2006, ISBN 4061586211.

== Under the Ritsuryō System == Udoneri was one of the [[official titles]] under the [[Ritsuryō system]] and belonged to the {{ill|Ministry of the Center|en|Ministry of the Center|WD=Q6867690|lt=Ministry of the Center|1=Ministry of the Center}}. In the ''[[Wamyō Ruijushō]]'', it is recorded as "'''Uchitoneri'''". Their station was located at the northeast corner of the Ministry of the Center. Initially, the number of Udoneri was fixed at 90, but this was reduced to 40 in {{ill|808|en|808|WD=Q399792|lt=808|1=808}} ([[:en:Daido|Daido]] 3). However, in {{ill|1148|en|1148|WD=Q19633|lt=1148|1=1148}} ([[:en:Kyuan|Kyuan]] 4), the number was increased to 60 to accommodate candidates applying through [[Seikō (appointment)|Seikō]], and by the late 12th century, over 100 individuals held this position (''[[East Asian government position Hishō]]''). Some Udoneri were selected to serve as [[:en:suitors|suitors]] (attendants) to the [[Regent (Japan)|Regent]] or [[:en:Kampaku|Kampaku]], referred to as '''Udoneri Zuishin'''. Candidates were chosen from sons of nobles Ranked between [[Fourth Rank]] and [[Fifth Rank]] who were aged 21 or older. Sons of nobles of the [[Third Rank]] or higher were appointed upon request without restrictions. Since Udoneri were treated as superior among officials of similar posts, their position provided advantages for career advancement.<ref name="wada74"/>

For example: - A [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] Udoneri was called '''Gen'nai'''. - A [[Taira clan|Taira]] Udoneri was called '''Heinai'''. - A [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara]] Udoneri was called '''Tōnai'''. - A {{ill|Tachibana clan|en|Tachibana clan|WD=Q7673718|lt=Tachibana|1=Tachibana clan}} Udoneri was called '''Kichinai'''. - A [[Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|Ki]] Udoneri was called '''Kinai'''. - A {{ill|Kiyohara clan|en|Kiyohara clan|WD=Q2975295|lt=Kiyohara|1=Kiyohara clan}} Udoneri was called '''Seinai'''. - A {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|Ōtomo clan|WD=Q1096662|lt=Ōtomo|1=Ōtomo clan}} Udoneri was called '''Ban'nai'''.
Later, these designations were used irrespective of the individual's actual position or surname.<ref name="wada74"/>
== Meiji Era and Beyond == In the [[Meiji]] era, Udoneri became part of the [[:en:Imperial Household Ministry|Imperial Household Ministry]]. Within this structure, they were under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Lord Steward|en|Lord Steward|WD=Q1547759|lt=Lord Steward|1=Lord Steward}} as part of the {{ill|Board of Chamberlains|en|Board of Chamberlains|WD=Q11453306|lt=Board of Chamberlains|1=Board of Chamberlains}}.
